w:r.w:t: LeadCode
Full Implementation Plan
Auto-push accepted solutions to GitHub • LinkedIn activity integration
Document Version: 1.0
Prepared: June 2026
1. Project Overview

LeadCode is a competitive coding platform — similar to LeetCode — with a critical differentiator: every accepted submission is automatically committed to the user's own GitHub repository and optionally shared as a LinkedIn activity post.
What makes LeadCode different
Users connect their GitHub and LinkedIn accounts once during onboarding.
When any solution is accepted, LeadCode auto-pushes the code to their GitHub repo, organized by difficulty and problem name.
Users can opt-in to auto-post their accepted solutions to LinkedIn, building a public coding portfolio passively.
Every submission is recorded — giving users a visible commit history that functions as a live resume artifact.
Core User Flow
User signs up and connects GitHub (required) and LinkedIn (optional).
User solves a problem in the Monaco code editor.
On "Submit", code is evaluated by the Judge0 engine against hidden test cases.
If verdict = Accepted: a background job fires to push code to GitHub + optionally post to LinkedIn.
User sees their accepted solutions in a profile page and via their GitHub commit history.
Phase 1 — Authentication & User Identity
This is the foundation. Everything else depends on a stable user identity with securely stored third-party OAuth tokens.
Step 1.1 — Core Auth Setup
Use Supabase Auth for email/password and magic link sign-in. Supabase handles JWT issuance, session refresh, and RLS (row-level security) on your Postgres database out of the box.
Database schema for the users table:
users: id (uuid), username, email, avatar_url, created_at, github_connected (bool), linkedin_connected (bool)
STEP
PROBLEM / CHALLENGE
FIX & IMPLEMENTATION
1.1 — Email Auth
Supabase magic links expire after 1 hour — users on slow networks may miss them and get frustrated.
Enable both magic link AND password auth so users can choose. Set magic link expiry to 24h in Supabase dashboard under Auth > Email settings.
1.2 — Session Persistence
Supabase sessions expire by default after 1 hour, forcing users to re-login mid-solve.
Enable "Persist sessions" in Supabase. Set JWT expiry to 7 days. Use the Supabase JS client's onAuthStateChange listener to silently refresh tokens.
Step 1.2 — GitHub OAuth Integration
GitHub OAuth is mandatory — it is the core feature of LeadCode. Users without GitHub connected cannot have their solutions saved automatically.
OAuth Scopes required:
repo — needed to create files and commits in the user's repository
user:email — needed to identify the user and link accounts
Full OAuth Flow:
Redirect user to: https://github.com/login/oauth/authorize?client_id=YOUR_ID&scope=repo,user:email&state=RANDOM_STATE
GitHub redirects back to your callback URL with ?code=XXX
Server-side: POST to https://github.com/login/oauth/access_token with code + client_secret
Parse the returned access_token from the response body
Encrypt and store the token in your DB against user_id
Call GET /user on the GitHub API to fetch their github_username
Prompt the user to select or create a GitHub repo for their solutions

// Exchange code for token (server-side only — never expose client_secret)const res = await fetch("https://github.com/login/oauth/access_token", { method: "POST", headers: { Accept: "application/json" }, body: JSON.stringify({ client_id, client_secret, code })});const { access_token } = await res.json();// Store encrypted: await encrypt(access_token) before saving to DB
STEP
PROBLEM / CHALLENGE
FIX & IMPLEMENTATION
1.2a — Scope Too Broad
The "repo" scope gives access to ALL user repos. Users may feel this is invasive and refuse to connect.

Clearly explain in the OAuth consent UI: "We only write to the specific repo you choose." Consider requesting the "public_repo" scope if users primarily want a public solutions repo — it is less invasive.
1.2b — Token Encryption
Storing plain GitHub access tokens in DB is a critical security risk. A DB breach exposes all user repos.
Encrypt tokens at rest using AES-256-GCM before storing. Use a server-side KMS key (AWS KMS or GCP KMS). Decrypt only in the background job worker when needed to make API calls.
1.2c — Token Revocation
User revokes the GitHub app manually. All subsequent pushes will fail silently with 401 errors.
Catch 401 responses from GitHub API in your worker. Mark github_connected = false and send the user an in-app notification: "GitHub connection expired — reconnect to resume auto-saves."
1.2d — Repo Selection
Users may not know which repo to link, or may accidentally pick a wrong one containing other code.
After OAuth, show a modal listing their existing repos (via GET /user/repos API). Also offer: "Create new repo called 'leadcode-solutions'" as the top option with a single click.
Step 1.3 — LinkedIn OAuth Integration
LinkedIn uses OAuth 2.0 with OpenID Connect. The w_member_social scope allows posting on behalf of the user. This requires LinkedIn to review and approve your app.
OAuth Scopes required:
openid — identity verification
profile — access to name and profile picture
w_member_social — permission to create posts on behalf of the user

// LinkedIn OAuth URLhttps://www.linkedin.com/oauth/v2/authorization ?response_type=code &client_id=YOUR_CLIENT_ID &redirect_uri=https://leadcode.app/auth/linkedin/callback &scope=openid%20profile%20w_member_social &state=RANDOM_STATE
STEP
PROBLEM / CHALLENGE
FIX & IMPLEMENTATION
1.3a — App Review Required
LinkedIn requires manual app review before granting w_member_social to other users. Can take 1-4 weeks.
Apply for app review immediately at developer.linkedin.com. In the meantime, launch with GitHub-only. LinkedIn posting can be a v2 feature. Use your own account for demos.
1.3b — Token Expiry
LinkedIn access tokens expire after 60 days. Refresh tokens also expire eventually. This breaks auto-posts silently.

Store token_expiry timestamp alongside the access_token. Before each post, check if expiry < now + 7 days. If so, attempt refresh. If refresh fails, mark linkedin_connected = false and notify the user.
1.3c — Post Spam
If a user solves 10 problems in one day, they get 10 LinkedIn posts. This is extremely annoying for their network.
Implement a daily digest option: batch all that day's accepted solutions into a single post at 8 PM. Also add a per-submission toggle: "Share this on LinkedIn" checkbox visible before submitting.
Phase 2 — Problem System & Code Execution Engine
The problem editor and code runner are the core product experience. Users need a fast, reliable in-browser coding environment with accurate verdict feedback.
Step 2.1 — Problem Database Schema

problems: id UUID primary key title TEXT -- e.g. "Two Sum" slug TEXT unique -- e.g. "two-sum" (used in GitHub folder path) difficulty ENUM(easy, medium, hard) tags TEXT[] -- e.g. ["array", "hash-table"] description TEXT -- HTML or Markdown starter_code JSONB -- { python: "...", js: "...", java: "..." } test_cases JSONB -- [{ input: "...", expected_output: "..." }] hidden_tests JSONB -- same format, not exposed to users
Step 2.2 — Monaco Code Editor (Frontend)
Monaco Editor is the same editor that powers VS Code. It provides syntax highlighting, IntelliSense, and keyboard shortcuts that developers already know.

npm install @monaco-editor/react// Usage in your React component:import Editor from "@monaco-editor/react";<Editor height="500px" language={selectedLanguage} // "python", "javascript", etc. value={code} onChange={(val) => setCode(val)} theme="vs-dark"/>
STEP
PROBLEM / CHALLENGE
FIX & IMPLEMENTATION
2.2 — Editor Load Time
Monaco Editor is a large bundle (~2MB). First load is slow on mobile connections.
Use dynamic import with Next.js: const Editor = dynamic(() => import("@monaco-editor/react"), { ssr: false }). Monaco loads only when the problem page is visited, not on the homepage.
2.2 — Language Switching
When user switches language mid-solve, their written code gets wiped, causing frustration.

Store code per-language in a state object: { python: "...", javascript: "..." }. When switching, save current code to that language key. Restore when switching back. Persist in localStorage as a draft.
Step 2.3 — Judge0 Code Execution Engine
Judge0 is an open-source code execution system that runs submitted code in isolated Docker containers. It supports 60+ programming languages and can be self-hosted or used via their cloud API.
Self-hosting Judge0 (recommended for production):
Deploy Judge0 on a separate server (Railway or Fly.io) using their Docker Compose setup.
Judge0 uses RabbitMQ for job queuing and Redis for caching — both are included in the compose file.
Point your Next.js API route to your Judge0 instance URL.
Never expose Judge0 directly to the browser — always proxy through your own API.

// Submit code to Judge0 (from your API route, not client)const submission = await fetch("https://your-judge0.railway.app/submissions", { method: "POST", headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" }, body: JSON.stringify({ language_id: 71, // 71 = Python 3, 63 = JavaScript source_code: btoa(code), // base64 encode stdin: btoa(testInput), expected_output: btoa(expectedOutput) })});const { token } = await submission.json();// Poll GET /submissions/{token} until status is not "Processing"
STEP
PROBLEM / CHALLENGE
FIX & IMPLEMENTATION
2.3a — Polling Latency
Polling Judge0 every second for results adds unnecessary load and feels slow to the user.
Use exponential backoff polling: check at 500ms, 1s, 2s, 4s. Show a real-time spinner with elapsed time counter so users know the system is working. Most submissions return in under 2 seconds.
2.3b — Cheating via stdin
Users can print the expected_output directly using sys.stdin tricks to fake acceptance.

Never send expected_output to Judge0 from the client. Run all hidden tests server-side. Compare outputs server-side. The client only ever sees "Accepted" or "Wrong Answer" — never the expected output for hidden tests.
2.3c — Resource Abuse
A user can submit infinite loops or memory-exhausting code, crashing your Judge0 instance.
Set Judge0 limits: cpu_time_limit=5 (seconds), memory_limit=131072 (128MB). Rate-limit submissions per user: max 10 per minute. Add a run queue so bursts don't crash the server.
2.3d — Language Coverage
Adding support for a new language requires updating your problem starter code templates, test runners, and file extension mappings.
Create a languages config file: { id: 71, name: "Python 3", ext: "py", judge0_id: 71 }. All language-specific logic references this config. Adding a new language means adding one entry to this file.
Phase 3 — GitHub Auto-Push on Accepted Submission
This is the defining feature of LeadCode. When a submission is accepted, the code is committed to the user's GitHub repo automatically — no manual copy-paste required.
Step 3.1 — Repository Folder Structure
Solutions are organized in a consistent folder structure so the GitHub repo stays clean and browsable:

leadcode-solutions/ README.md ← auto-generated profile page solutions/ easy/ two-sum/ solution.py README.md ← problem description + your stats medium/ longest-substring/ solution.js README.md hard/ median-of-two-sorted-arrays/ solution.java README.md
Step 3.2 — GitHub Contents API (No Git CLI Needed)
You do NOT need to run git commands on your server. GitHub's REST API has a Contents endpoint that creates and updates files directly:

// Create or update a file via GitHub APIconst path = `solutions/${difficulty}/${slug}/solution.${ext}`;const url = `https://api.github.com/repos/${username}/${repo}/contents/${path}`;// First check if file exists to get its SHA (required for updates)const existing = await fetch(url, { headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${githubToken}` }}).then(r => r.ok ? r.json() : null);// Then create or updateawait fetch(url, { method: "PUT", headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${githubToken}`, "Content-Type": "application/json" }, body: JSON.stringify({ message: `✅ Solved: ${title} [${difficulty}] — LeadCode`, content: Buffer.from(code).toString("base64"), sha: existing?.sha // omit if creating new file })});
STEP
PROBLEM / CHALLENGE
FIX & IMPLEMENTATION
3.2a — Commit Author
The commit will show as authored by your app's GitHub bot account, not the user. Their GitHub contribution graph won't update.
In the Contents API request, add an "author" field: { name: userGithubUsername, email: userEmail }. GitHub will attribute the commit to the user, and it will count toward their contribution graph.
3.2b — File Already Exists
If a user solves the same problem again (to improve their solution), the PUT will fail without a SHA.

Always do a GET before PUT. If GET returns 200, extract the sha field from the response. Pass sha in the PUT body. If GET returns 404, omit sha — it's a new file. Handle both cases in a helper function.
3.2c — Auto-generated README
Raw code files in GitHub are hard to browse. There is no context about what the problem was.

Auto-generate a README.md alongside each solution file containing: problem title, difficulty, link to LeadCode problem, submission date, runtime and memory stats. Push both files in separate API calls (GitHub Contents API does not support batch commits).
3.2d — Rate Limits
GitHub API allows 5000 requests/hour per token. A power user solving many problems fast could hit this.

Track API usage per user token. GitHub returns rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining). If remaining < 50, queue the push for the next hour instead of failing. Show user: "GitHub push queued — will appear shortly."
Phase 4 — LinkedIn Auto-Post on Accepted Submission
LinkedIn posting is opt-in and controlled entirely by the user. The goal is to help users build a public presence without spamming their connections.
Step 4.1 — LinkedIn UGC Posts API

// Post to LinkedIn on behalf of userawait fetch("https://api.linkedin.com/v2/ugcPosts", { method: "POST", headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${linkedinToken}`, "Content-Type": "application/json", "X-Restli-Protocol-Version": "2.0.0" }, body: JSON.stringify({ author: `urn:li:person:${linkedinUrn}`, lifecycleState: "PUBLISHED", specificContent: { "com.linkedin.ugc.ShareContent": { shareCommentary: { text: `Just solved "${title}" on LeadCode! 🚀\nDifficulty: ${difficulty} | Language: ${language}\nView solution: ${githubLink} #coding #${difficulty} #LeadCode` }, shareMediaCategory: "NONE" } }, visibility: { "com.linkedin.ugc.MemberNetworkVisibility": "PUBLIC" } })});
Step 4.2 — User Controls for LinkedIn Posting
Always give users full control. Never auto-post without explicit consent:
Global toggle in Settings: "Auto-post accepted solutions to LinkedIn" (default: OFF)
Per-submission checkbox in the submit dialog: "Share this on LinkedIn"
Daily digest mode: instead of one post per problem, batch into one end-of-day summary
Cooldown setting: "Only post if I solve at least 3 problems today" — avoids single-problem posts
STEP
PROBLEM / CHALLENGE
FIX & IMPLEMENTATION
4.1a — UGC API Deprecated
LinkedIn is gradually deprecating the UGC Posts API in favor of Posts API (v2). Using the wrong endpoint can cause sudden failures.

Check LinkedIn developer changelog at the time of implementation. As of 2025, ugcPosts still works but monitor for deprecation notices. Abstract the post function so swapping the endpoint requires changing one file.
4.1b — Post Duplicate
If the background job retries due to a timeout, the same solution could get posted to LinkedIn twice.

Before posting, write a pending_post record to DB with a unique key (user_id + problem_id + submission_id). Use a DB unique constraint. The job checks this before posting. Mark as posted=true after success.
4.1c — Token Expired Mid-Post
LinkedIn tokens expire at 60 days. If the access_token expires, the post silently fails.

Wrap every LinkedIn API call in a try-catch. On 401, attempt token refresh using the refresh_token. If refresh also fails, set linkedin_connected = false and send user notification. Never swallow the error silently.
Phase 5 — Settings & Connections UI
The Connections settings page is where users manage their GitHub and LinkedIn integrations. It needs to be clear, trustworthy, and give full control.
Step 5.1 — Connections Page Layout
Each connected service should show: connection status, connected account name, what permissions were granted, action buttons to change or disconnect, and auto-push settings.
GitHub Section:
Status badge: "Connected as @username" (green) or "Not connected" (gray)
Target repo selector: shows currently selected repo with a "Change" button
Checkbox: "Auto-push to GitHub on accepted submissions" (default: ON once connected)
Checkbox: "Auto-generate README per solution" (default: ON)
"Disconnect" button with confirmation dialog
LinkedIn Section:
Status badge: "Connected as Full Name" or "Not connected"
Checkbox: "Auto-post to LinkedIn on accepted submissions" (default: OFF)
Dropdown: "Post frequency" — Immediately / Daily digest at 8 PM / Never
"Disconnect" button
STEP
PROBLEM / CHALLENGE
FIX & IMPLEMENTATION
5.1 — Disconnect Consequences
If a user disconnects GitHub mid-session, pending background jobs may try to push using the now-invalid token.

When user disconnects, immediately set github_connected = false and delete the stored token. Jobs check this flag before running. If false, skip the push and log: "Push skipped — GitHub disconnected."
5.2 — Repo Change
User changes their target GitHub repo. Previous solutions were pushed to the old repo. New solutions go to the new repo. The history is split.

When user changes repo, show a warning: "Previous solutions pushed to [old-repo] will not be migrated automatically. Only future solutions will go to [new-repo]." Optionally offer a "Migrate all solutions" button as a background job.
Phase 6 — Background Job Queue
GitHub pushes and LinkedIn posts must NOT block the submission response. They run asynchronously in a job queue so the user gets their verdict instantly.
Step 6.1 — Why a Job Queue?

Without a queue: the HTTP request waits for GitHub API + LinkedIn API before returning a response to the user. This adds 1-3 seconds of latency on every accepted submission, and if either API is down, the submission response fails entirely.
With a queue: the submission returns "Accepted" in under 200ms. The GitHub and LinkedIn tasks run in the background within seconds. Failures can be retried without affecting the user experience.
Step 6.2 — Inngest Setup (Recommended)
Inngest is a serverless-friendly event queue that works natively with Next.js and Vercel. No Redis or RabbitMQ server needed.

// Define the job (in inngest/functions.ts)export const onSubmissionAccepted = inngest.createFunction( { id: "on-submission-accepted", retries: 3 }, { event: "submission/accepted" }, async ({ event, step }) => { const { userId, problemId, code, language, submissionId } = event.data; await step.run("push-to-github", async () => { await pushToGitHub({ userId, problemId, code, language }); }); await step.run("post-to-linkedin", async () => { await postToLinkedIn({ userId, problemId, submissionId }); }); });// Trigger from your API route after verdict:await inngest.send({ name: "submission/accepted", data: { userId, problemId, code, language, submissionId }});
STEP
PROBLEM / CHALLENGE
FIX & IMPLEMENTATION
6.1 — Job Failure
GitHub API returns 500. The push fails. User never knows their solution was not saved.
Inngest retries automatically (3x with exponential backoff). After all retries fail, write a failed_push record to DB and show an in-app notification: "GitHub push failed for [problem]. Retry now?"
6.2 — Duplicate Jobs
Network timeout causes the job to be enqueued twice for the same submission. User gets two GitHub commits and two LinkedIn posts.
Use submissionId as the Inngest event ID (idempotency key). Inngest deduplicates events with the same ID within a 24-hour window. This ensures exactly-once execution per submission.
6.3 — Queue Monitoring
Background jobs are invisible. You have no idea if they are succeeding or silently failing at scale.
Inngest provides a dashboard at app.inngest.com showing all events, function runs, and retry history. Set up alerting on failure rate > 5% using their webhook notifications to your Slack or Discord.
Phase 7 — User Profile & Submission History
Every user gets a public profile page showing their solved problems, streak, and a link to their GitHub solutions repo. This is their live coding portfolio.
Step 7.1 — Profile Page Features
Total problems solved, broken down by difficulty (Easy / Medium / Hard)
Current solving streak (days) with a heatmap calendar (similar to GitHub contributions)
Language distribution chart — how many problems solved in each language
Link to their GitHub solutions repo with a star count
Recent accepted solutions list with problem name, difficulty, language, and timestamp
Public shareable URL: leadcode.app/@username
Step 7.2 — Submission History Page
All submissions — accepted and rejected — with full code and verdict
Filter by: problem, language, verdict, date range
Click any submission to see the code, test case results, and runtime/memory stats
"Push to GitHub" manual button for accepted submissions where auto-push failed
STEP
PROBLEM / CHALLENGE
FIX & IMPLEMENTATION
7.1 — Public Profile Privacy
Some users do not want their solving activity publicly visible.
Add a "Profile visibility" toggle in settings: Public / Private. Private profiles show only to the logged-in user. Default to Public since the GitHub integration already makes solutions public.
7.2 — Streak Calculation
If a user is in UTC+5:30 and solves at 11:45 PM, the next solve at 12:15 AM (same calendar day for them) breaks the streak by UTC calculation.
Store user timezone at signup. Calculate streaks in the user's local timezone, not UTC. Use the date-fns-tz library for reliable timezone-aware date arithmetic.
8. Full Technology Stack
Recommended technology choices for building LeadCode from zero to production:
LAYER
TECHNOLOGY
REASON
Frontend
Next.js 14 (App Router)
Server components for fast first load, API routes for backend logic, easy Vercel deployment.
Styling
Tailwind CSS
Utility-first, no context-switching between files, great for building a consistent design system quickly.
Code Editor
Monaco Editor (React)
Same editor as VS Code. Developers are already familiar with it. Supports 60+ languages.
Auth
Supabase Auth
Built-in OAuth, magic links, JWT, and row-level security. Works with Next.js out of the box.
Database
PostgreSQL (Supabase)
Relational, supports JSONB for test cases, and Supabase gives you a free managed Postgres instance.
ORM
Prisma
Type-safe DB queries, auto-generated client, easy migrations. Works with Supabase Postgres.
Code Execution
Judge0 (self-hosted)
Open source, supports 60+ languages, runs code in isolated Docker containers, free to self-host.
Job Queue
Inngest
Serverless-friendly, native Next.js integration, built-in retry logic, visual dashboard, no Redis needed.
GitHub Integration
GitHub OAuth App + REST API v3
Contents API allows file creation without running git CLI. No extra infrastructure.
LinkedIn Integration
LinkedIn OAuth 2.0 + UGC Posts API
Official API for posting on behalf of users. Requires app review for w_member_social scope.
Hosting (Frontend)
Vercel
Zero-config Next.js deployment, edge functions, automatic HTTPS, global CDN.
Hosting (Judge0)
Railway or Fly.io
One-click Docker Compose deploy. Judge0 needs its own isolated server for security.
9. Recommended Build Order
Build in this sequence to ship a working core quickly and layer features on top:
Week 1-2 — Foundation
Next.js project setup + Tailwind + Prisma + Supabase Auth (email login)
Problems table seeded with 10-20 sample problems
Monaco Editor integrated on the problem detail page
Judge0 deployed and connected — "Run" button working
Week 3-4 — Core Submission Flow
"Submit" button with full Judge0 verdict (Accepted / Wrong Answer / TLE)
Submissions table recording all attempts
Basic user profile page showing solved count
Week 5-6 — GitHub Integration (Core Differentiator)
GitHub OAuth flow — connect GitHub account
Repo selection modal
Inngest job queue setup
Auto-push to GitHub on accepted submission
Settings page — GitHub section
Week 7-8 — LinkedIn + Polish
LinkedIn OAuth flow (pending app review — use your own account for testing)
LinkedIn post job in Inngest
Per-submission LinkedIn toggle
Full profile page with heatmap and GitHub repo link
Submission history page
Error handling for failed pushes with in-app notifications
Post-Launch
Streak system + daily email digests
Leaderboard per problem (fastest runtime, least memory)
Problem submission by community (user-created problems)
VS Code extension to submit directly from local editor
10. Critical Security Reminders
⚠ CRITICAL
Never store GitHub or LinkedIn tokens in plain text. Always encrypt at rest with AES-256-GCM.
⚠ CRITICAL
Never expose your GitHub OAuth client_secret or LinkedIn client_secret in frontend code. Token exchange must happen server-side only.
⚠ CRITICAL
Never send expected test case outputs to the browser. All verdict comparisons happen server-side.
⚠ CRITICAL
Judge0 must run on a separate server from your main app. Never allow user code to run on the same machine as your database or API.
✅ GOOD
Rate-limit all API endpoints. Max 10 code submissions per user per minute. Max 100 API requests per user per hour.
✅ GOOD
Use Inngest idempotency keys to prevent duplicate GitHub commits or LinkedIn posts on job retries.
LeadCode Implementation Plan — End of Document
